steps.prompt: Is there a finite verb in this sentence?
steps.reveal: Domine, ecce gladii duo hic [sunt] = Lord, behold, two swords are here

hints.yes — 'ecce' is a verb: 'Ecce' is an interjection meaning 'behold/look!' — it is not a conjugated verb
hints.yes — 'hic' is a verb: 'Hic' is an adverb meaning 'here' — it is not a verb
hints.yes — 'Domine' is a verb: 'Domine' is a noun in the vocative — it means 'Lord!' (direct address)

steps.prompt: What verb is understood (implied) in this sentence?

hints.est (is): 'Est' is 3rd singular — 'gladii duo' is plural, requiring 'sunt'
hints.habent (they have): 'Habent' would require a subject — the context implies 'gladii sunt hic' (swords are here)
hints.veniunt (they come): The swords are not coming — they are present here; 'sunt' is understood

hints.edo, esse, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edo' (to eat)
hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the parts of 'eo' (to go)
hints.habeo, habere, habui, habitum: These are the parts of 'habeo' (to have)

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'Dominus' — 'Domine' with -e ending is vocative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'Domino' — 'Domine' is vocative (direct address)

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'domini' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'domini' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'domini' ends in -i

hints.genitive singular: Genitive singular 'gladii' looks the same, but context (with 'duo') shows nominative plural
hints.vocative plural: Vocative plural 'gladii' looks the same, but they are not being addressed
hints.dative singular: Dative singular would be 'gladio' — 'gladii' ends in -ii

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'gladii' ends in -ii
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'gladii' ends in -ii
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'gladii' ends in -ii

hints.ablative: Ablative of 'duo' would be 'duobus' — 'duo' is nominative
hints.accusative: Accusative masculine would be 'duos' — 'duo' is nominative here

hints.adverb: An adverb modifies a verb — 'ecce' is an interjection calling attention
hints.conjunction: A conjunction joins clauses — 'ecce' is an exclamatory interjection
hints.imperative verb: 'Ecce' resembles a command but is classified as an interjection, not a verb form

steps.prompt: What part of speech is 'hic' in this sentence?

hints.demonstrative pronoun (this): 'Hic' as a pronoun means 'this one', but here it means 'here' (adverb of place)
hints.adjective (this): 'Hic' as an adjective modifies a noun, but here it stands alone as an adverb
hints.conjunction: 'Hic' is never a conjunction — it is either a pronoun/adjective or an adverb

translation: Lord, behold, here are two swords
